1.blurb: The Sasanian Empire — last of the great pre-Islamic Iranian dynasties — had ruled from Ctesiphon since 224 CE and was, by 600, the eastern superpower of the late antique world. The most recent Roman-Persian war (602–628) under Khosrow II had drained both empires; the Arab armies that erupted from Arabia after Muhammad's death would destroy the Sasanian state in less than two decades. The Persian cultural tradition, however, would outlast the conquest and reshape the Abbasid Caliphate that followed.

The Byzantine Empire under Heraclius (610–641) was both the great Christian power of the late antique world and the empire that the Arab armies were about to dismantle. Heraclius had just won the most spectacular comeback of late antique military history — defeating the Sasanians at Nineveh in 627 and recovering the True Cross — but the war had bankrupted Byzantium and depopulated its eastern provinces. The Yarmouk catastrophe of 636 took everything Heraclius had recovered.

The Christian Kingdom of Aksum had occupied Yemen briefly in the early sixth century after the Jewish king Dhu Nuwas's persecution of Yemeni Christians; the Aksumite governor Abraha's expedition against Mecca around 570 (the Year of the Elephant in Arab tradition) is the cross-cultural connection that brings Aksum into the early Islamic narrative. The Aksumite negus would shortly shelter the first Muslim refugees from Meccan persecution.

The collapse of the Western Roman Empire in the fifth century had left the Mediterranean political map of the late antique world in the hands of the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) state and the Sasanian Persians. The Arab conquests of the seventh century would be the second great reorganization of that map — replacing two ancient empires with one new one over half their combined territory in a single generation.
Pre-Islamic Arabia sat between the two exhausted superpowers — Sassanid Persia and Byzantium — whose mutual ruin opened the door for the conquests.
The 7th-century world Islam erupted into had just seen Gupta India shattered too — the classical order collapsing on every front.

2.blurb: Heraclius's twenty-year war against the Sasanians (Byzantine Empire Ch 3) and his triumphant return of the True Cross to Jerusalem in 630 — the same year as Muhammad's conquest of Mecca — was the high point of Byzantine recovery. Six years later, Heraclius would lose Syria to the army Muhammad had built. The two greatest religious-political accomplishments of 630 happened on opposite sides of the same Arabian frontier and would shortly meet in catastrophe for one of them.

The Tang Dynasty was founded in 618, four years before the Hijra, and would become the cultural and military superpower of seventh-century Eurasia. The Tang and the early Caliphate were the two great expanding powers of the period; their armies would eventually meet on the Talas River in 751, just after the Abbasid Revolution destroyed the Umayyads. Until then, the two empires expanded toward each other from opposite ends of Eurasia without either side fully understanding what the other was.

The Asuka period in Japan (538–710) saw the formal adoption of Buddhism as state religion, the introduction of Chinese-style centralized administration under the Taika reforms (645), and the construction of the first major Buddhist temples on the Japanese archipelago. While Muhammad was preaching at Mecca and his successors were conquering Persia, the Japanese state was being reorganized along continental Asian lines under Korean and Chinese influence.
Muhammad's lifetime overlapped the catastrophic last Byzantine-Sassanid war — the context that made the conquests possible.
Within a generation of Muhammad's death the Sassanid Persian Empire would simply cease to exist — absorbed entirely into the Caliphate.

3.blurb: Qadisiyyah in 636, three months after Yarmouk, destroyed the Sasanian field army and effectively ended the Persian Empire as a political institution. The Sasanian commander Rostam Farrokhzad was killed; the war elephants panicked in a sandstorm; the formation broke. Within a year the Sasanian capital at Ctesiphon had fallen, and within fifteen years the last Sasanian king Yazdegerd III had been murdered at Merv. The destruction of Sassanid Persia was the most rapid imperial collapse in late antique history.

Yarmouk in August 636 ended Roman power in Syria after seven hundred years. Khalid ibn al-Walid's tactical brilliance defeated a numerically superior Byzantine army under Vahan in difficult terrain, and within two years Damascus, Antioch, and Jerusalem had all fallen to the Arabs. Heraclius spent his final years in mental and physical decline, watching the empire he had recovered from the Sasanians be lost again to a new enemy.

The Coptic Christians of Egypt had been Monophysites under heavy Constantinopolitan persecution since the Council of Chalcedon in 451 — two centuries of theological harassment that the Arabs ended overnight. The Copts did not resist the Arab conquest with any enthusiasm; their bishops negotiated terms; they accepted the dhimmi arrangement and kept their religion. Egypt became Arabic-speaking and Muslim very gradually over the following centuries, but the political-administrative shift was instantaneous.

The Sawad — the irrigated grain belt of southern Iraq, the most productive farmland in the medieval world — had been the agricultural foundation of every Mesopotamian state from Sumer through the Sasanians (Mesopotamia Ch 13). The Arabs inherited it intact, used it to feed their new garrison cities at Kufa and Basra, and would later use it to feed Abbasid Baghdad. The Sasanian throne room and Spring of Khosrow carpet that fell to the Arabs at Ctesiphon in 636 marked the end of Mesopotamia's pre-Islamic political continuity.
The Rashidun conquests are antiquity's closest analogue to the Mongol explosion — a peripheral people overrunning the settled superpowers with stunning speed.
The Arab armies reached the Indus within decades — knocking on the door of the post-Gupta Indian world.

4.blurb: The Sunni-Shia split that crystallized at Karbala in 680 has its closest structural analogue in the Christological controversies of the fifth and sixth centuries — disputes over Christ's nature that produced the Chalcedonian, Monophysite, and Nestorian traditions of Christianity. Both Islam and Christianity, in their first centuries, produced foundational schisms whose theological substance was real but whose political driver was a contested succession. The Christian schisms produced the Orthodox-Catholic-Oriental divide; the Muslim schism produced Sunni-Shia. Both are still operative in 2026.

The conversion of Iran from Sunni to Twelver Shia under the Safavid dynasty in 1501 — eight centuries after Karbala — was the moment the Karbala-shaped Shia tradition got a major state to call home. Safavid Iran became the institutional anchor of Twelver Shia Islam, with the imported Lebanese clerics from Jabal Amil training the Iranian clerical class that eventually produced the marja-i taqlid system, Ayatollah Khomeini, and the Islamic Republic of 1979. The thread from Karbala to Tehran runs through the Safavid revolution.

The 1258 Mongol sack of Baghdad ended the Abbasid Caliphate after five centuries and opened a period in which Sunni-Shia conflict was less politically structured. Hulagu Khan's army destroyed the institutional center of Sunni political authority that the Abbasid Revolution had built; the Twelver Shia clerical tradition that had developed in opposition to the Abbasids carried on at the shrine cities of Najaf and Karbala under various successor regimes. The Mongols, of course, were not aware they were creating space for Shia institutional development; the consequence was structural.

The 1979 Islamic Revolution in Iran was the moment when the Twelver Shia clerical hierarchy that the Safavids had built — and that the Karbala memory had given its emotional vocabulary — became the ruling political institution of a major modern state. The chain runs Karbala 680 → Safavid Iran 1501 → late Safavid Usuli mujtahids → Qajar marja-i taqlid system → Khomeini → the current Islamic Republic. Modern Iran is, institutionally, the most direct heir of the Karbala-shaped Shia tradition.
The Sunni-Shia split born here would, nine centuries on, become the Safavid state religion — the fault line still defining the region.

5.blurb: Mu'awiya's choice to keep his capital at Damascus rather than returning to Medina meant running the new caliphate through inherited Byzantine institutions — Greek-speaking civil servants, Roman tax registers, late antique urban infrastructure. The Umayyad state was, structurally, a Byzantine afterlife under Arab management. Abd al-Malik's Arabization reform of 695 began the long replacement of this Greek-administrative substrate with Arabic — a process that took a generation but locked Arabic in as the language of the Middle East.

The Umayyad gold dinar of 696 deliberately replaced the Byzantine solidi and Sasanian drachms — the two great imperial coins of late antiquity — with a new Islamic currency that bore Arabic Quranic inscriptions and no human imagery. The coinage reform was a sovereignty statement and a religious one simultaneously. The dinar would dominate medieval Mediterranean and Middle Eastern commerce for the next four centuries.

The Persian-speaking population of post-conquest Iraq and Iran was the social base from which the Abbasid Revolution would eventually draw its strength. Under the Umayyads they were second-class mawali Muslims paying disproportionate taxes; under the Abbasids they would become the senior administrative class of the new caliphate, carrying Sasanian governance traditions into the Islamic state. The continuity of Persian administrative culture across the regime change of 651 and again across the regime change of 750 is the through-line of post-Sasanian Iranian history.
Umayyad Damascus ran the largest empire on earth while northern Europe was still pre-Viking — the civilizational gap was vast.
The Umayyad century is contemporary with the Maya Postclassic reorganization — two unconnected worlds reshaping at once.

6.blurb: The Visigothic Kingdom that Tariq ibn Ziyad destroyed in 711 was the post-Roman successor state in Iberia, founded by Germanic peoples who had taken Spain from the disintegrating Western Roman Empire in the late fifth century. The Visigoths had ruled Spain for over two centuries when the Arabs arrived, but their elective monarchy was structurally fragile and their unified state collapsed almost instantly under Arab pressure. Spain went from post-Roman Christian rule to Muslim rule in a single year.

The 751 Battle of Talas, fought just after the Abbasid Revolution that ended the Umayyad dynasty, was the moment when the Arab and Tang Chinese expansions of the previous century finally collided directly on the Talas River in modern Kyrgyzstan. The Abbasid-led army defeated the Tang force, and Central Asia was Islamicized rather than Sinicized for the next thousand years. Talas falls just outside the Umayyad period, but the Arab-Tang trajectories that brought it about are the natural conclusion of the Umayyad eastern conquests.

Muhammad ibn al-Qasim's 711 conquest of Sind was the first major Muslim foothold on the Indian subcontinent, more than a century after the Gupta Empire had collapsed and several centuries before the Delhi Sultanate would consolidate Muslim political rule over northern India. The Sind conquest is the founding moment of Indian Islam; the long process by which Muslim rule would extend across the subcontinent — and by which Indian Islamic civilization would develop its distinctive synthesis with Hindu and Buddhist cultures — was beginning here.

The 717–718 siege ended in Arab catastrophe — Greek fire, the Theodosian Walls, an unusually severe winter, and a Bulgarian intervention from the north combining to destroy the largest Arab military operation ever launched against the Byzantine capital. Of 1,800 Arab ships, only five returned to the Caliphate. Leo III, who had taken the Byzantine throne five months before the siege began, became the founder of the Iconoclast period and saved Christian eastern Europe from absorption into the Caliphate.
The conquest of Visigothic Spain extinguished the last western-Roman successor kingdom — 711 finishing what 476 began.

7.blurb: The Temple Mount platform that Abd al-Malik chose for the Dome of the Rock in 691 had been the site of the Jewish Second Temple destroyed by the Romans in 70 CE. The platform had been abandoned for over six centuries — Christianity having concentrated its religious building at the Holy Sepulchre across the city. The Umayyad decision to build the Dome of the Rock and al-Aqsa on the abandoned Temple Mount made Jerusalem the third-holiest city in Islam and locked in the layered Jewish-Christian-Muslim sacred geography that has shaped the city ever since.

The Damascus mosque site had hosted a temple of the Aramean storm god Hadad since at least the Iron Age, then a Roman temple of Jupiter, then a Christian basilica of John the Baptist, before becoming a mosque under al-Walid I. Aramaic — the lingua franca of the Levant and Mesopotamia for over a thousand years — would be displaced from everyday use over the following centuries by Arabic, persisting only in liturgical use among Eastern Christian churches and a few isolated villages. The Aramean cultural layer beneath Damascus is one of the deepest substrates of Levantine history.

The Byzantine iconoclast movement that began under Leo III in 726 — only a few decades after the Dome of the Rock — was partly inspired by what Byzantine elites had observed of Islamic aniconism. The Umayyad refusal to use human or animal imagery on coins or in religious buildings impressed some Byzantine observers as a return to authentic monotheistic discipline. The eighth-century Byzantine iconoclast controversy is one of the few cases where Islamic theological practice influenced Christian theological practice rather than the other way around.

The dhimmi system that the Umayyads codified for Christian, Jewish, and Zoroastrian subjects ran for thirteen centuries — from the seventh-century Arab conquest through the late nineteenth-century Ottoman tanzimat reforms. Jewish communities under Islamic rule generally fared substantially better than Jewish communities in contemporary Christian Europe, particularly in the medieval Mediterranean. The protected-minority arrangement was not equality, but it was a workable framework that allowed Jewish religious and communal life to continue under Islamic political authority.
The Dome of the Rock was a deliberate architectural answer to Byzantine Christianity — Islam announcing itself in Jerusalem's skyline.
The Dome stands on the Temple Mount — Umayyad Islam inscribing itself onto the sacred geography of ancient Israel.
The Abbasid revolution that ended the Umayyads recurs as a pattern — the caliphate's legitimacy crisis the Ottomans would one day inherit and claim.

8.blurb: The Abbasid Revolution that destroyed the Umayyad dynasty in 750 is also the founding moment of the Islamic Golden Age. The new dynasty's capital at Baghdad (founded 762), its Persianized administration through the Barmakid family, its sponsorship of the Translation Movement, and its institutional support for Islamic scholarship would produce five centuries of intellectual flowering. The narrative continues directly into Islamic Golden Age Ch 1.

Baghdad, founded by the Abbasid caliph al-Mansur in 762 north of the ruined Sasanian capital at Ctesiphon, was a perfect circle 1.5 miles in diameter — the first major city in human history that was purely Islamic from the foundation stones up. The Round City would shortly become the seat of the most productive intellectual workshop in the medieval world, the cultural center of the Abbasid Caliphate, and the eventual victim of the 1258 Mongol sack that ended the institutional Islamic Golden Age.

The Translation Movement that began in scattered late Umayyad form and exploded under Abbasid patronage was the central intellectual project of medieval Islamic civilization. Greek philosophical, mathematical, medical, and astronomical texts were translated into Arabic at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad — and centuries later re-translated from Arabic into Latin to seed the European Renaissance. The chain Athens → Sasanian Gundishapur → Abbasid Baghdad → medieval Toledo → Renaissance Europe runs through the project that picked up steam under the Abbasids.

The Mongol sack of Baghdad in 1258 ended the institutional Abbasid Caliphate after five centuries — exactly half a millennium of continuity from the Umayyad-Abbasid transition of 750. Hulagu Khan's army destroyed the House of Wisdom, killed the last effective Abbasid caliph al-Musta'sim by reportedly rolling him in a carpet and trampling him with horses, and effectively ended the central institutional life of medieval Islamic civilization. The buildings, the scholarly traditions, and the dynastic afterlife in Mamluk Cairo all survived; the institutional center did not.

The Fatimid Caliphate (909–1171), an Ismaili Shia state that conquered Egypt and ruled from Cairo, was one of the major Shia counter-caliphates that emerged in opposition to the Sunni Abbasids. The Fatimids built al-Azhar in Cairo (founded 970), still the world's oldest continuously operating Islamic university, and patronized one of the great cosmopolitan medieval cultures. The Sunni-Shia geopolitical division that the Karbala killing had institutionalized produced its most successful Shia state experiment in Fatimid Egypt.
